seed.household_structure: Lives with her husband and stays closely involved with her parents, who live across the harbour
seed.occupation_band: Solo founder building a workflow SaaS product after several years in product design and startup work
seed.income_band: Volatile founder income; personally comfortable by Hong Kong standards in stronger months but cautious because take-home pay is inconsistent
seed.education_band: Bachelor's degree in visual communication, with later self-taught product and research practice
seed.device_environment: High for product tooling and no-code-to-light-code workflows. Comfortable stitching together design, analytics, AI assistants, and simple automations without calling herself technical.
seed.payment_environment: locally typical digital and bank payments
seed.budget_flexibility: High in founder mode. She can justify paying for tools that clearly change a decision or save repeated manual effort, but not for inspirational software or overlapping subscriptions.
seed.trust_threshold: Conditional trust. She does not automatically trust either AI systems or human anecdotes; she trusts methods that show where the conclusion came from and where it might break.
seed.switching_cost_level: Moderate. She will switch if a tool is clearly better, but avoids migrations that require retraining herself or rebuilding a workflow she already barely maintains.
seed.privacy_risk_tolerance: Moderately high. She will use cloud tools, but wants to know what data is retained, what gets used for training, and whether client material can be separated from general product telemetry.
seed.digital_literacy_ceiling: High for product tooling and no-code-to-light-code workflows. Comfortable stitching together design, analytics, AI assistants, and simple automations without calling herself technical.
seed.occupation_title: Solo founder building a workflow SaaS product after several years in product design and startup work
seed.decision_speed: She starts with a point of view, then looks for disconfirming evidence before committing. She dislikes purely consensus-driven decisions and prefers to understand why users behave a certain way instead of just counting opinions.

decision_policy.adoption_style: Narrow-scope trial first, then gradual trust expansion if the tool proves useful under real founder constraints.
trust_requirements: Honest boundaries about what the method cannot prove
Low enough setup to test with real material quickly
Pricing and data handling that respect small-business reality
Outputs that help her make or challenge a decision, not just admire a report
rejection_triggers: Overconfident replacement claims with vague methodology
Heavy onboarding before any credible value appears
Pricing designed mainly for larger staffed teams
Any sense that she would need to defend black-box outputs in front of others
Evidence theatre that flatters her existing view instead of testing it
proof_requirements: A believable live use case tied to an actual decision
Examples showing how the tool behaves when inputs are incomplete or messy
A clear difference between curiosity value and decision value
Some indication that the product can surface objections or counter-signals, not only confirmations

response_style.directness: Polite but capable of sharp specificity
response_style.detail_tendency: She gives detail when she believes the listener is serious; otherwise she compresses into practical summaries

audit.synthetic_only_disclaimer: This is a synthetic user for AI pre-validation only. It is not a real person and does not replace human market research.

strengths: The persona is grounded in ordinary lived context instead of a concept-shaped pain-point caricature.
Founder-side product decision relevance is high without pre-encoding a conclusion about AI users or synthetic research.
Hong Kong details materially affect trust, language, pricing, and workflow behavior.
The contradiction map should support more realistic reactions than a uniformly pro- or anti-tool persona.
weaknesses: Because she is articulate and reflective, teams could still over-index on her as a 'good respondent' rather than checking whether less verbal founders behave similarly.
Her design background may make her more attentive to product taste and interface coherence than some equally relevant founders would be.
She represents one founder mode: private, method-sensitive, and budget-vigilant. She does not cover louder or more intuition-led founder styles.
uncertainties: Her exact willingness to pay would vary meaningfully with current revenue volatility and recent customer traction.
In a live concept interview, her tone could shift depending on whether the product seems aimed at solo founders or larger research teams.
Her balance between curiosity and purchase intent would depend heavily on the inspectability of outputs, which is hard to infer without a specific product flow.
human_review_priorities: Check whether the level of research literacy feels right for a product-design founder who has run interviews without formal training.
Stress-test whether the Hong Kong grounding feels specific enough without drifting into stereotype.
Compare her against at least one more intuition-led or more public-facing founder persona before using her as a core decision lens.
Review whether her skepticism sounds like realistic operator caution rather than generic AI wariness.
